title: Finger marbling on plain water
subtitle: Oil-free, size-free marbling using diluted acrylic paint and a finger
excerpt: Finger marbling, dropping diluted acrylic paint onto plain water and pulling patterns through with a finger or stylus, is the simplest of the marbling techniques. It produces looser, less defined patterns than carrageenan-bath marbling but is accessible with minimal equipment. The technique is closest in character to the spontaneous marbled endpapers found in nineteenth-century pocket books.
sourceNotes: The marbling tradition in Europe is documented in Charles Woolnough, The Whole Art of Marbling (1853, 2nd ed. 1881), a practical manual that describes the basic floating-paint technique that underlies all Western marbling practice. Woolnough's account predates carrageenan and describes plain-water marbling with oil-based colours. The plain-water acrylic technique adapts this approach using modern materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: Brushing a solution of potash alum onto paper before marbling. The alum bonds with the acrylic pigment and prevents the colour from sliding off when the paper is lifted from the size. Without mordanting, the pattern may rinse away when the sheet is removed from the tray.
Paint that remains on the surface of water rather than sinking or dispersing. In marbling, the surface tension of the water supports the paint and allows it to be shaped before transfer to paper.

A second print taken from the tray immediately after the first, using the residual paint remaining on the surface. Ghost prints are lighter and less defined than the first print; they are useful as backgrounds for writing or collage.

content.text: technique requires no specialist size or thickener, the paint floats on the surface tension of plain water. The patterns are more random than carrageenan-bath marbling; the trade-off is that set-up is ten minutes rather than twenty-four hours.

items.name: Liquid acrylic marbling colour (pre-thinned), two or three colours
items.substitutions: Artists' acrylic thinned to 50% water by volume with a drop of ox-gall per 10 ml to help it float

items.name: Potash alum dissolved in warm water, 1 tablespoon per 500 ml
items.qty: enough to brush one side of each sheet

content.text: Brush the alum solution evenly over the writing face of each sheet. Allow to dry completely before marbling, at least 30 minutes. Only the mordanted face will pick up the pattern; the back of the paper will be clean.
Fill the marbling tray with clean water to a depth of 3 to 4 cm. Let the water settle for 2 minutes so there is no movement on the surface.

Drop paint onto the water surface from an eyedropper or the end of a stirrer, one drop at a time. Each drop should spread into a disc about 3 to 5 cm across. If it sinks, the paint is too thick; thin with more water. If it spreads instantly to nothing, add a drop of ox-gall to the paint and try again.

Pull your finger or a stylus through the floating colours to create veins and swirls. Work quickly, the paint is mobile on the water surface for about 1 to 2 minutes before it begins to dry at the edges.
Lay the mordanted paper face-down onto the surface in one smooth motion. Hold the edges and lower from one end. Leave for 30 seconds, then lift.
Rinse the marbled sheet gently under slow-running cold water to remove excess alum. Dry flat.
After lifting the first print, the residual paint remaining on the water surface produces a ghost print. Lay a second sheet immediately and lift for a softer, secondary pattern.

items.symptom: Paint sinks to the bottom instead of floating
items.cause: Paint is too thick, too heavy-bodied, or does not have enough surfactant.
items.fix: Thin further with water. Add a drop of ox-gall to the paint. Use pre-thinned liquid marbling acrylic rather than studio-grade acrylic.

items.symptom: Pattern rinses off when washing the sheet
items.cause: The paper was not mordanted, or the alum solution was too dilute.
items.fix: Re-mordant the paper with a stronger alum solution and allow to dry fully before the next attempt.
